speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I remember we were packing. We were trying to get as many things-- of course, in those days, you didn't have cars. You didn't have trunks. You had wagons. My mother had gotten one of those wagons that you pull.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheelbarrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheelbarrow wagons, the little ones that you pull. And whatever we could, that's what we were going to put on there. So we had a lot of food left and a lot of things left in the apartment. And my mother said to my-- to her girlfriend, she said, why don't you-- and they used to call me Gitta. Why don't you and Gitta go and take all these things to your mother. Her mother lived in the old part of the city. And take it to her so that she can make use of it. And I will wait here for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother's girlfriend and I are now across the city, far away from my mother. And suddenly, you hear explosions. You hear bombs. You hear machine gun fire. And the city started to explode.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were scared to death. And I said, oh, my God. You know, I started to cry. I've got to get back to Mommy. Mommy, Mommy, Mommy. So we went out, and we started to walk. And there were tanks that came through the city streets with megaphones. And they said, any civilians that are displaced, that need to get home to another place, they can walk with their hands up, and nothing will happen to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started walking across the city with our hands up. But the fighting got so fierce-- I mean, it's almost like when you see the movies today. You're walking, and there are soldiers running either side of you, and there are machine guns, and there's grenades exploding. And you hear airplanes. And the tanks are rolling on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a horrible situation. People were dying left and right of you. And we came to the Platz. I forgot what the name of it is, the major, major artery at the center of the city where all the government buildings were. And that's where the German headquarters were, where the German seat was. And my mother's girlfriend said to me, now look. She says, you and I are going to walk in there. I'm going to tell them that I'm your governess and that you have been displaced. One of the things I forgot to say that helped me throughout this war is that I did not look Jewish. So I could-- what-- how do you say when you try to disappear--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: --into the crowd. There's a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: You blend in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blend in. So I blended in. And I spoke fluent German, of course. She said, you are going in there. And I'm going to tell them that I'm your governess.

speaker: interviewer
text: At headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: At headquarters. And that they need to take care of you. And everything will be all right. And you tell them. In the beginning-- see-- the beginning of the war, my mother told me that when we were all separating, and these horrible things were happening, that they made a vow that if anybody survived, and if we could get together, that we would get back to Berlin. That would be our central point to try to get back to the same place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she takes me in there, and she tells them, and they fall for the story. They take her away. I never saw her again. They took me, and I think I must have been there three, four days. They treated me royally.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fighting was fierce. I would sit with the officers on the balcony and watch the city burn to the ground all around me. I mean, the-- the city was on fire. Everything was burning. And the soldiers were coming in and out, and they were looting the stores. And some of them would bring me beautiful things.

speaker: interviewer
text: They thought of you as the German girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, I was what? 10 years, 11 years old or something like that. They would bring me beautiful things. And I remember one of them brought me a ring. And I swear to this day, it must have been like a 10, 15-carat diamond ring. It was a clear, beautiful stone in a gold setting. And I wore it all the time. And I'll tell you what happened to that. But anyway, they treated me royally. During that time, the Germans rounded up all the civilians and all the people that were left in the city and sent them into work camps, into Germany. And I would stand by the window every day because they were like-- the lines were three deep. They were being processed in the courtyard. And I was waiting to find my mother. I would look for my mother. But I never, ever saw her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever scared that they would find out about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was so many other things to be scared about. I was scared that a bomb would fall on this building, and-- and I would die. That's what I was scared of every day, the machine guns and the dying. I mean, they-- they would-- you have no idea. I watched them bring in the officers. There was one time they brought in a car, an officer's car, with the Germans. And one of them was sitting in the front seat, and all his brains were blown out. And I mean, this is what I saw, and this is what I witnessed. And that, to me, was scary. I didn't, you know, what, where, why, how, you know, in those days. Anyhow, finally, they said, do I know anybody. Is there any way-- a caravan was leaving to go to the train the next day to leave Warsaw, to go back to Germany. And would I know, do I know anybody, any relatives that I could contact, that they could send me to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they made contact with my relatives-- my-- my grandmother, who was with a friend. And my-- well, my aunt was there, too. And they sent me with a private nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it to this day. There was a four-car caravan that drove through the city. There were snipers on the roofs. There were cars-- the car behind us got totally blown up. Another car got-- people were wounded, and the car had to stop and leave. Two cars out of the four made it to the train station. And they put me on a train. And we stopped in Lodz, which was at that time Litzmannstadt. And now it's back to Lodz. And we stopped there. I guess-- I don't know. They had to-- they had to-- All-- all I know is we were there all day. And we stopped for lunch in a building. And I had to go to the bathroom. And I took the ring off. And I washed my hands. And I left [LAUGHS] the ring in the bathroom. And I didn't realize it until after we had already gotten on the next train to go to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what happened to the ring. Whoever found the ring, you know, was in A-good shape. And I-- I'm sorry I left it there. [LAUGHS] But and then, I was reunited with my grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in the meantime, my grandmother went to a small village. She had very close friends who had a huge estate in a city called Belzec, which was-- if you equate it to Los Angeles, I guess, if you go from here to Palm Springs. Because I know it was, you know, about that kind of a distance. And she stayed there with her-- with these people. They had this huge estate. And my aunt was there, too. And that's where we lived. And they had tremendous-- there were about five or six barracks of people that were work camp, people from-- from Russia, from Poland. And all these people that were rounded up, you know, they didn't put in concentration camps. They put in work camps to work the estates. And in the meantime, now, my mother gets picked up and put into one of the work camps. She-- but for whatever reason, she must have come through after I left Warsaw because I didn't see her in the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you suppose that your mother was told by the other woman what had happened to you? Or did she just think that you had disappeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think the other woman was taken away and put in work camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother thought you were lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, yes. Oh, yes. Well, that, I'll tell you about that later. Anyway, my mother gets sent to this work camp, which is way somewhere else in Germany. She makes contact with the people where my grandmother was staying. They in turn request-- they say they need more workers. They can't handle the load. And they requested workers from the camp where my mother was. And they requested my mother to be among them. Don't ask me how. Maybe they told them that they heard this woman was a good worker, or that she's got relatives here in this-- in-- in my place. I don't know how. But my mother ended up coming to be with us. And I'll never forget it because in those days, you had to walk. And she walked from the train station in Belzec out to this estate, which was another maybe 10, five miles or something like that. And we went to meet her. And I'll never forget this. I saw this-- see this woman coming in the distance towards me. And it's my mother. And that's how I got reunited with my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we're all at this estate. And the war, every day, we would watch the planes go into Berlin to do their bombing. I mean, this was like a constant humming. You never forget that humming. There were like 20, 30 planes at a time that would fly across-- Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- they must have been Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians, Americans, English, British, I don't know who they were. But they were coming to bomb. And it was a terrible time because, you know, even though we were safe there, we never knew how long we were going to be safe. And one day, somebody in the village-- this estate was in a small village. Somebody came through the village and said that the Russians are coming close. The Russians are coming tomorrow. And whoever wants to leave, if they're-- if they're afraid, they can leave. And the people, the very people who helped us stay alive, who owned this estate, packed up their wagons with their horses. And I remember, there were three almost like covered wagons because they had the tarp over the wagons. They loaded up whatever they could, and we all got in the wagons. And we started to leave the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, my mother said, wait a minute. Why-- why-- why are we leaving? We don't have to leave. Let's stay. I don't-- I don't want to go with them. If the Russians are coming, they're going to liberate us. We're going to be free. So I remember, my grandmother and my mother and I, we get off. And we are now-- the three-- there were four other people in this-- in the house and on the estate. We stayed. And we heard all these horrible stories that the Russians, when they come through with their tanks, they would have dead bodies hanging from the front of the tanks, and they would do horrible things to you, and they would-- you know, it would-- they-- all these terrible stories. Well, some of them were true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, there was like this dead silence came over the village, the night. We were all waiting for something. We were listening for tanks. We were listening. And the next day, suddenly, I heard this clop, clop, clop, clop, clop, clop. They came on horses. There were four or five of them. Very quietly came through the center of the village and just looked around. They didn't do anything. And then half an hour, an hour later, the group came. The motorcycles came. The trucks came. Everything came. And let me tell you something. I-- first of all, they interrogated us. They wanted to know who we were. So we told them. Because at that time, I could still speak Polish very well, and so did my mother. And we said, we're Jews, and we are Poles. And they said, ah, good good. You know, And they left us alone. And they told us we're fine, you know, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. The next day, I-- I had been out playing somewhere. I come back, and I hear screaming. I hear my mother is screaming. And I run up the stairs. And there is one of the Russian soldiers attacking her, wanting to rape her. And I remember, I started beating on him and beating on him. And I started yelling. And luckily, one of the officers came and pulled him away and really gave him a thrashing because every day-- now, you have to understand, I was 12 years old, maybe. Behind the kitchen window of this house was an herb garden, a vegetable garden, a big garden. Almost every day, the Russians would come with the motorcycles, with the girls, and rape the girls in that garden. And I watched them rape the women. That's how I learned about sex. And so they thought that they could get after my mother, too. My mother was a beautiful woman. After this guy did this, that night, we locked our door. And we heard he came back. He sat outside that door the whole night, hoping and waiting that maybe we would come out. Anyway, so the Russians in those days weren't so wonderful. They had their own agenda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to rape you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's the strangest thing. They didn't try. They-- nobody ever tried to come near me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find your father again?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The war ends. And lots of horrible things happened in those times, you know, in the village, where after the Russians and after the war ended, we stayed there for a little bit. And there was a lot of wild ammunition lying around. And kids started playing with ammunition. And I saw one young boy pick up a grenade. And it exploded and just blew up his stomach. And these kids, you know, there are a lot of kids who died because they wanted to play with this stuff, and they didn't know not to play with it. There were a lot of horrible things that went on. But we decided-- when we heard through the Russians that Berlin was clear, that all the armed forces had finally taken over Berlin, and that the war was over, we decided to go back to Berlin. And again, we had this little wagon. And I mean, whatever we had left. And I remember, it took us maybe two days. We walked the Autobahn because there was no transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you pulled the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: We pulled the wagon. We took out turns. We pulled the wagon. Well, we were all younger then. You know, I mean, I was young. [LAUGHS] And we pulled the wagon all the way into Berlin. We walked. We walked the Autobahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the wagon by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a little bit of food and mostly sweaters. I know my mother had a suitcase, one or two suitcases. And whatever she could put it in there. I think she must have had some mementos because miraculously, through all this, I have photographs that were saved from place to place to place. So those must have been, you know, the valuables that she thought she needed to take.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go to Berlin. And we try and get settled. I just thought about this the other day. I can't remember. What did my mother do for money all this time? Because we ended up in a little apartment. I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, the Joint had set up an office. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And everybody that was displaced or was looking for relatives, they wanted people to register. So my-- we went there, and we found my aunt. But my mother in her excitement forgot to register.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- we went to my aunt first, and we connected with my aunt. And naturally, we were thrilled that she was alive. We found out-- found out that my uncle was killed in Auschwitz, but she survived. And we rented this apartment. And we were going to go back and re-register. In the meantime, my father, in the village that he was-- in the beginning, if you want to back track. He joined-- it got too-- too scary and too dangerous for him to stay in the village, so he went into the woods and joined the partisans. And when the war ended, rejoined-- I guess, was it the Russian army or the Polish army? I don't know if they were together or what. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he came to Warsaw. And he tried to find my mother and me. And somebody who knew us told him that they know that my mother is dead. They are pretty sure that she was killed. But they think that I got out. And maybe he could find me. So my father goes on a pass, or whatever. You know, he comes to Berlin to find us. The first thing that happens to him, he gets mugged and robbed by Russian soldiers. They took all his money. They took his watch. He had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he found out about the center where people register. And he found my aunt's name. And he connected with my aunt. And he said, do you know anything about Ann and Gitta? And she said, yes, they're here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we were in this little apartment. And I remember so well. My father walked through the door. He walked through the door, and he had a bottle of Coca-Cola in his hand. And I mean, it-- it was like-- like seeing God. I mean, I could not believe that here he was was, after all this time. I would get to see my father. And this bottle of Coca-Cola, that's all I remember. And then he put it on the table. And I used to take just little sips because I didn't want to-- I didn't want it to end, and I didn't want it to empty. So I just-- almost like licking a-- a piece of candy. And that's-- that's how we all got back together, the three of us. And I tell you, the one thing I forgot to tell you, the-- the tremendous courage of my mother. Because if I told you in the beginning, through all of this, my mother was a brittle diabetic and was very, very ill. Could not get proper insulin. Could not keep her proper diet. And there were several times during that period where I almost lost her, where she died-- almost died because she was in a coma. She would end up in a deep diabetic coma. And I remember in Warsaw one time, I was sitting on the bed. And the doctor said to me, don't let her go to sleep. Keep slapping her face. You have to keep slapping her face. And I remember, I would slap my mother's face until she finally started coming out of the coma. And she just-- she had such tremendous strength to go through and do all the things that she did to be able to live and work for people that-- that-- that killed us, the Germans. But we needed to eat. And there were times I think she even talked that if she would ever have the chance, maybe she herself could be one of those people that could do a little turning in or whatever. But that never happened. She was too scared to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turning in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, well, she worked. Remember, I told you, she worked for the Germans. She worked for an officer's club, and she was a waitress. And many times, she thought that maybe she would give some information to the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. Turning the-- the-- the Germans into the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And but-- but she-- it was too scary. She was-- she had me there, and she didn't want to-- to jeopardize that. So that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you were, the three of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Together again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father begin again? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father started to-- well, we ended up-- naturally, we lived together in the apartment. And my father started to make music again. And the interesting thing was that right after the war in Berlin, most of the Jews that were there were the Polish Jews and the Russian Jews and the European Jews. And he became the music for our-- for this community. Because even though all these years, he kept his fluent Yiddish. He spoke Yiddish fluently. And because of the-- some of the Russian Jews and some of the Polish Jews, that they-- we would always communicate in Yiddish. And I think it's very interesting. I just thought of that the other day because as a young man, he was totally unreligious and had nothing to do with his Jewish background or anything. You know, he was the enfant terrible. He was out there having a great time. And all of a sudden, he turned back to his roots because it was very important for him to make Jewish music. And he composed a lot of Yiddish songs. And he played-- I mean, his fiddle, he played beautifully. And he made some records. And I have-- unfortunately, I waited too long. You know what the materials were like in Berlin after the war. I have about 10 recordings that he made. But they were on this rubberized-- this really early, early, you know, material that they used. And over the years, it just deteriorated. And I have-- I finally put it on tape, but I should have done it years ago because it's a little scratchy, and it's a little not clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did save it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I saved it. I saved all his music. I saved all his compositions. And he-- one of the first gatherings of the Jews in Berlin after the war was a commemoration of the Warsaw Ghetto, of the people who died in the ghetto. And I remember, we all gathered in this huge theater. It had a balcony. There were people upstairs, downstairs. And it was a commemorative evening. And my father composed a song that evening. [SPEAKS YIDDISH] Anyway, he just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember more of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I have it here. I have the-- I have the-- the-- the text. And he-- he was just an incredible man. Both my parents were. They were-- they were strong. They were giving. They were talented. Because of my mother's diabetes, she couldn't have any more children, and then of course, the war. So I remained an only child. And we just we're so glad, the three of us, to be together and-- and have-- have a life again. And that's what my father did. He-- he played his music. He had an orchestra. He played for all the Jewish weddings that happened after the end of the war, the young people falling in love and getting married. And-- he-- There were a lot of very, very religious weddings because the-- the European Jews, I remember, there was never a wedding where the bride wasn't walked around the chuppah. I forgot how many times you do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven times. And he just-- he tried. I know he-- he bought a nightclub on the [SPEAKS GERMAN]. And he tried that. And that failed. He lost some of the money that he had invested in it. And that didn't work out. And one of his friends-- in those days, there was a lot of black market going on. And all the entrepreneurs who had come out of the camps and out of hiding were making a lot of money with the connect of the-- the Americans, the cigarettes, the liquor, the everything. A lot of money was made.

speaker: interviewee
text: Silk stockings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Silk stockings. And one of my father's--
